_meta.authoringNotes: Global burden figures per Costa et al. PLoS Negl Trop Dis 2015 (PMID 26379143) — estimated 1.03 million cases and 58,900 deaths annually. Pathophysiology and clinical syndromes per Levett PN. Clin Microbiol Rev 2001 (PMID 11292640), Bharti et al. Lancet Infect Dis 2003 (PMID 14652202), and Karpagam & Ganesh. Eur J Clin Microbiol Infect Dis 2020 (PMID 32449116). Diagnostic algorithm per WHO/ILS 2003 guidelines and Picardeau et al. Diagn Microbiol Infect Dis 2014. Treatment per Suputtamongkol et al. Clin Infect Dis 2004 (PMID 15578370) and Brett-Major & Coldren Cochrane 2012. Chemoprophylaxis per Sehgal et al. Natl Med J India 2000. Pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome data from Andes region (Sandes-Sotomayor et al.) and Hong Kong. Indian-specific data from ICMR-NIE Chennai surveillance and Kerala Directorate of Health Services monsoon-season reports.

h1Title: Leptospirosis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Leptospirosis is a bacterial zoonotic disease caused by spirochetes of the genus Leptospira, acquired through contact with water, soil, or food contaminated by the urine of infected animals — most often rodents but also cattle, pigs, dogs, and wildlife. WHO estimates more than 1 million severe cases and around 58,900 deaths each year, with peaks during the monsoon season across South and Southeast Asia, the Caribbean, Latin America, and tropical Africa. The clinical spectrum runs from a self-limited flu-like illness (around 90% of cases) to severe Weil's disease with jaundice, acute kidney injury, and hemorrhage, and to a rapidly fatal pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome with mortality above 50%. Onset is typically 5-14 days after exposure, with abrupt fever, severe headache, intense calf myalgia, and conjunctival suffusion — a highly characteristic finding. Early oral doxycycline or intravenous ceftriaxone shortens illness in mild cases and reduces mortality in severe disease; supportive care with renal replacement and mechanical ventilation is essential in advanced presentations. Risk peaks among rice farmers, fishermen, sewer workers, slum residents during floods, and travellers who do water sports in fresh water.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 1.03 million severe cases per year globally; 58,900 deaths per year (Costa et al. 2015)
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly men aged 20-49 in occupational and recreational water exposure; flood-related epidemics affect all ages and sexes
keyStats.avgAge: Working-age adults dominate; outbreaks during floods affect children and elderly equally
keyStats.globalCases: Mortality 5-10% overall; up to 40% in Weil's disease and over 50% in pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome

definition: Leptospirosis (ICD-10: A27) is a systemic zoonotic infection caused by pathogenic spirochetes of the genus Leptospira (most relevantly L. interrogans sensu lato, including over 250 serovars across roughly 25 serogroups). Animal reservoirs (rodents are the principal source; cattle, pigs, dogs, and wildlife are also competent hosts) carry the spirochete chronically in the renal tubules and shed live organisms in urine for months to years. Humans are accidentally infected when broken or hydrated skin, mucous membranes, or conjunctivae come into contact with contaminated freshwater, mud, animal urine, or rarely tissues. After an incubation of 2-30 days (typically 5-14), the illness is biphasic: a septicemic phase of 4-7 days in which leptospires circulate in blood and cerebrospinal fluid produces high fever, severe headache, myalgia, conjunctival suffusion, photophobia, abdominal pain, and a transient rash; a brief defervescence is followed by an immune phase with antibody-mediated complications including aseptic meningitis, uveitis, jaundice, acute kidney injury, pulmonary hemorrhage, and bleeding diatheses. Approximately 90% of cases are mild and anicteric and resolve spontaneously; 5-10% progress to Weil's disease (icteric leptospirosis with renal failure and bleeding) or pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome. The disease is managed by infectious disease, tropical medicine, internal medicine, nephrology, and critical care services, depending on severity.

typesClassification.description: Approximately 90% of cases. Self-limited febrile illness lasting 5-7 days with headache, myalgia, conjunctival suffusion, and gastrointestinal symptoms. Often misdiagnosed as influenza, dengue, or undifferentiated viral fever, particularly during monsoon outbreaks.

5-10% of cases. Classic triad of jaundice, acute kidney injury, and hemorrhage. Mortality 5-40% even with optimal care. Hyperbilirubinemia is conjugated; renal failure is non-oliguric tubulointerstitial with hypokalemia.

Emerging severe form first recognized in epidemics in the Andes, Nicaragua, India (Andaman & Nicobar), and Korea. Sudden onset of dyspnea, hemoptysis, and respiratory failure with hypoxemia despite normal chest auscultation early. Mortality 50-75% even with intensive care.
Aseptic meningitis is a common feature of the immune phase, with neck stiffness, photophobia, CSF lymphocytosis, and normal glucose. Rare cases progress to encephalitis or transverse myelitis.
Late immune complication appearing weeks to months after the acute infection. Causes anterior uveitis, vitritis, and rarely panuveitis. Responds to topical corticosteroids and mydriatics.

primarySymptoms: Sudden onset of high fever (39-40 °C) with rigors and severe frontal or retro-orbital headache 5-14 days after potential exposure to floodwater, mud, or animal urine.
Intense myalgia, especially in the calves and lower back, frequently described as the most severe pain the patient has experienced — a near-pathognomonic feature.
Conjunctival suffusion: bilateral non-purulent redness of the bulbar conjunctivae without exudate or itch, appearing on day 2-4 in 30-90% of cases.
Severe nausea, vomiting, and right-upper-quadrant abdominal pain mimicking acute hepatitis or surgical abdomen.
Cough, sore throat, and a transient maculopapular or petechial rash in roughly 10-25% of patients.
Photophobia, neck stiffness, and altered mental status in patients with leptospiral meningitis.
Progressive jaundice from day 4-7, often deep yellow because of conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, in Weil's disease.
Reduced urine output, swelling, and rising creatinine from acute kidney injury — often non-oliguric and hypokalemic.
Mucosal bleeding (epistaxis, gum bleeding, conjunctival haemorrhage), petechiae, ecchymoses, and gastrointestinal bleeding in severe disease.
Hemoptysis, dyspnea, and hypoxia signalling pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome — a critical-care emergency.
Eye pain, redness, and blurred vision weeks after the acute illness — leptospiral uveitis.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden high fever with severe myalgia 5-14 days after monsoon flooding, swimming in rivers, or contact with rodent-infested environments
Bilateral conjunctival redness without discharge in a patient with febrile illness in an endemic region
Mildly raised serum creatinine, hypokalemia, and elevated CK in a febrile farmer, fisherman, or sewer worker
Persistent jaundice without prior chronic liver disease in a patient with fever
Sudden onset of cough or breathlessness in a febrile patient from a flood-affected area — pulmonary hemorrhage may follow
emergencySigns: Hemoptysis, dyspnea, hypoxia, or alveolar infiltrates on chest imaging — pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome requiring immediate intensive care
Hypotension, severe oliguria, hyperkalemia, or uraemia — needs renal replacement therapy
Massive gastrointestinal hemorrhage or disseminated intravascular coagulation
Altered consciousness, seizures, or focal neurologic deficits suggesting encephalitis or stroke
Severe Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction after first antibiotic dose — chills, fever, hypotension, worsening myalgia

causes.cause: Contact with water or soil contaminated with infected animal urine
causes.description: Pathogenic Leptospira shed in animal urine survive in warm, moist soil and freshwater for weeks. Walking barefoot in flooded streets, wading through paddy fields, swimming in rivers or lakes, or handling animal carcasses without protection are the dominant exposure routes.

causes.cause: Direct contact with reservoir animals or their tissues
causes.description: Farmers, slaughterhouse workers, veterinarians, butchers, and abattoir staff are exposed to cattle, pigs, sheep, and dogs that may be asymptomatic carriers. Bite-related transmission from rats and dogs is rare but documented.

Rice and sugarcane farmers, fishermen, sewer and drainage workers, dairy workers, soldiers, garbage collectors, and rescue personnel during floods all face elevated occupational risk. PPE and prophylactic doxycycline reduce risk substantially.

Triathlons, swimming, kayaking, rafting, jungle hiking, military exercises, and adventure tourism in tropical regions are documented exposures. Travelers returning from Borneo Eco-Challenge events and Sabah white-water rafting have triggered some of the largest published outbreaks.

Slum environments with dense rodent populations, open drains, and recurrent flooding create high-risk urban niches. Salvador (Brazil), Mumbai, Manila, and Jakarta have recurrent post-monsoon outbreaks. Climate-change-related extreme rainfall is expected to intensify urban transmission.

causes.cause: Person-to-person transmission via blood, breast milk, or sexual contact
causes.description: Extremely uncommon. Human-to-human spread has been reported in rare circumstances (breast milk, sexual transmission, transplacental). The dominant route is environmental.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational exposure to water, soil, or animals
riskFactors.description: Rice farmers, fishermen, abattoir and slaughterhouse workers, sewer workers, drainage and sanitation staff, veterinarians, dairy workers, and military personnel in field operations carry the highest occupational risk.

riskFactors.factor: Monsoon flooding and urban slum residence
riskFactors.description: Post-monsoon urban flooding in Mumbai, Chennai, Manila, Jakarta, and Salvador triggers explosive outbreaks. Risk is concentrated in low-lying slums with poor drainage and dense rodent populations.

riskFactors.factor: Recreational freshwater activities in tropical regions
riskFactors.description: Triathlons, swimming, rafting, kayaking, jungle hiking, and adventure tourism with mud and water contact. Saltwater and properly chlorinated pools do not transmit infection.

riskFactors.factor: Contact with rodents and rodent-infested dwellings
riskFactors.description: Rats are the dominant urban reservoir; chronic asymptomatic renal carriage produces continuous urinary shedding. Rodent control through poisoning, trapping, and waste management reduces local transmission.
Cuts, abrasions, ulcers, or macerated skin allow spirochetes to enter. Healthy, dry, intact skin is relatively resistant; mucous membranes (mouth, conjunctiva) are highly susceptible.
Men predominate in most case series — typically 70-85% — reflecting occupational exposure patterns rather than inherent sex difference in susceptibility.
Modelling and surveillance data link rising case counts in Southeast Asia, the Caribbean, and Pacific to increased frequency of cyclones, monsoon flooding, and urban inundation.

affectedDemographics: Tropical and subtropical regions bear over 90% of the global burden — India, the Philippines, Indonesia, Thailand, Vietnam, Sri Lanka, the Caribbean, Brazil, Peru, and Ecuador.
Indian states with the heaviest documented burden include Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andaman & Nicobar Islands, Maharashtra, and Gujarat during monsoon months.
Outbreaks have followed major hurricanes and floods: Hurricane Mitch (1998, Central America), Typhoon Ondoy (2009, Philippines), Mumbai flood (2005), Salvador (Brazil), and Puerto Rico (2017).
Military exposures: US, UK, and French military have recorded outbreaks among troops on jungle training in Hawaii, Panama, and Malaysia.
Travellers: imported cases concentrate among returning eco-adventure tourists from Southeast Asia, Latin America, and the Pacific.
diagnosisOverview: Clinical suspicion is the foundation of diagnosis — fever, severe headache, calf myalgia, and conjunctival suffusion after relevant exposure should always prompt leptospirosis testing in endemic regions. Routine investigations show thrombocytopenia, normal or slightly elevated white-cell count with neutrophilia, elevated transaminases (typically less than 5× upper limit; greater rise suggests viral hepatitis), conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, elevated creatine kinase out of proportion to muscle injury, hyponatremia, and hypokalemia. Specific tests include serology, molecular tests, and culture. The microscopic agglutination test (MAT) is the reference standard — a fourfold rise in titre between acute and convalescent sera (3-7 days apart) confirms the diagnosis, but MAT requires a reference laboratory with a live antigen panel. IgM ELISA is the most widely used screening test, with sensitivity 60-95% depending on day of illness; sensitivity is low in the first 5 days. Rapid lateral-flow IgM cassettes (Leptotek, SD Bioline) are useful in field settings. PCR on blood within the first week of symptoms (then on urine and CSF in the second week) is the most useful early test and is rapidly replacing culture, which takes weeks. Real-time PCR targeting lipL32 or 16S rRNA is highly specific and sensitive 75-95% in the early septicemic phase. Culture in Ellinghausen-McCullough-Johnson-Harris (EMJH) medium remains the most definitive test but takes 4-12 weeks and is not clinically useful in real time. Differential diagnosis includes malaria, dengue, scrub typhus, hepatitis A and E, typhoid, hantavirus, viral hemorrhagic fevers, and meningococcal sepsis; in tropical regions multiple infections may coexist, particularly during outbreaks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for recent or current leptospirosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; result within 24-48 hours. Sensitivity rises after day 5-7 and reaches 90-95% by day 10. A negative result in the first 5 days does not exclude leptospirosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reference standard for serological diagnosis with serogroup identification
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Acute and convalescent sera (3-7 days apart) sent to a reference laboratory. A fourfold rise in titre or single titre above 1:800 confirms diagnosis. Result within 1-2 weeks; not routinely available in most district hospitals.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects leptospiral DNA in blood (first week of symptoms), urine (second week), and CSF; useful for early diagnosis when serology is still negative
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood, urine, or CSF sample sent to a reference laboratory; result within 1-3 days. Most useful when collected within 7 days of symptom onset.

diagnosticTests.test: Rapid lateral-flow IgM cassette (Leptotek, SD Bioline)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Point-of-care screening in field outbreak settings
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Finger-prick blood applied to cassette; result in 15-20 minutes. Sensitivity and specificity vary; positive results require confirmation by ELISA or PCR.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, liver function, renal function, and creatine kinase
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects characteristic laboratory pattern (thrombocytopenia, conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, raised creatinine, raised CK) and grades severity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; same-day results in most hospitals. Helps distinguish leptospirosis from viral hepatitis (where transaminases usually exceed bilirubin).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects alveolar infiltrates and hemorrhage in pulmonary leptospirosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard imaging; rapid result. Bilateral mid- and lower-zone patchy infiltrates suggest pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome.

treatmentOverview: Treatment combines prompt antibiotic therapy with aggressive supportive care for severe disease. For mild-to-moderate leptospirosis, oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days is the standard first-line regimen; amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily or azithromycin 500 mg daily for 7 days is used in children, pregnancy, and doxycycline intolerance. Treatment is most effective when started within 5 days of symptom onset, but is still given later because both clinical course and shedding are shortened. For severe leptospirosis (Weil's disease, pulmonary hemorrhage, meningoencephalitis, sepsis), intravenous penicillin G 1.5 million units every 6 hours, ceftriaxone 1-2 g once daily, or cefotaxime 1 g every 6 hours for 7 days is preferred; meta-analysis (Suputtamongkol CID 2004, PMID 15578370) showed equivalent outcomes between penicillin and ceftriaxone. The Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (chills, fever, hypotension, worsening myalgia within hours of the first antibiotic dose) is reported in roughly 9-22% and managed with intravenous fluids, antipyretics, and short-course corticosteroids in severe cases. Aggressive supportive care is critical: intravenous crystalloid for hypovolemia, vasopressors for septic shock, renal replacement therapy (continuous or intermittent hemodialysis) for acute kidney injury and hyperkalemia, mechanical ventilation with lung-protective settings and possible ECMO for pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome, blood-product transfusion for thrombocytopenia and bleeding, and corticosteroid pulses (methylprednisolone 500 mg-1 g IV daily for 3 days) in pulmonary hemorrhage (uncertain evidence but widely used). Public-health response involves rodent control, source-water management, doxycycline post-exposure prophylaxis (200 mg orally once a week during prolonged high-risk exposure or 200 mg single dose post-exposure), animal vaccination (cattle, dogs), public-information campaigns at the start of monsoon season, and prompt reporting of confirmed cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days (mild leptospirosis)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral therapy for mild and moderate disease in non-pregnant adults and children over 8. Begin as soon as leptospirosis is suspected — do not wait for confirmation. Contraindicated in pregnancy and children under 8.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Shortens illness duration by 2-3 days; reduces shedding; modest mortality benefit in mild disease (Cochrane review 2012)

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous penicillin G 1.5 million units every 6 hours for 7 days (severe disease)
medicalTreatments.description: Historical mainstay for severe leptospirosis. Effective and inexpensive. May provoke a Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction within hours of the first dose; manage with intravenous fluids and antipyretics.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mortality reduction roughly 50% when started within 4 days of symptoms; equivalent to ceftriaxone in trials

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous ceftriaxone 1-2 g once daily for 7 days (severe disease)
medicalTreatments.description: Equally effective alternative to penicillin G; convenient once-daily dosing and broader-spectrum cover in cases with diagnostic uncertainty.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent mortality and recovery time to penicillin G (Suputtamongkol CID 2004, PMID 15578370)

medicalTreatments.name: Azithromycin 500 mg orally daily for 7 days
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative for doxycycline-intolerant or pregnant patients; macrolide activity against leptospires comparable to doxycycline.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior to doxycycline in randomized trials in non-severe disease

medicalTreatments.name: Amoxicillin 500 mg orally three times daily for 7 days
medicalTreatments.description: Used for children under 8 and during pregnancy. Inexpensive and widely available.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in mild-to-moderate disease; commonly used in pediatric and outpatient settings

medicalTreatments.name: Renal replacement therapy (continuous or intermittent hemodialysis)
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated for acute kidney injury with oliguria, fluid overload, hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, or uremia. Continuous renal replacement therapy preferred in hemodynamically unstable patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mortality reduction roughly 30-50% in patients with severe AKI receiving early dialysis

medicalTreatments.name: Methylprednisolone pulses (500 mg-1 g IV daily for 3 days) for pulmonary hemorrhage
medicalTreatments.description: Used in pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome to suppress immune-mediated alveolar injury, although evidence is mixed; treat in combination with antibiotics and ventilatory support.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Observational data suggest mortality reduction in severe pulmonary hemorrhage; randomized trial evidence inconclusive

medicalTreatments.name: Mechanical ventilation with lung-protective strategy / ECMO (pulmonary hemorrhage)
medicalTreatments.description: Low-tidal-volume mechanical ventilation, ARDS-network principles, and venovenous ECMO have been used in pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome with severe hypoxemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Survival 40-60% with structured ICU care; significantly worse with delayed presentation

alternativeTreatments.name: Plasmapheresis
alternativeTreatments.description: Reported in case series for refractory pulmonary hemorrhage or massive bleeding diatheses; not standard of care. Used when other therapies fail.

whySeeSpecialist: Suspected leptospirosis warrants prompt evaluation in an emergency or infectious disease service — outcomes are sharply better with antibiotics started within 4-5 days. Severe disease (jaundice, renal failure, pulmonary hemorrhage) requires intensive care with nephrology and pulmonology input.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an infectious disease or tropical medicine physician familiar with regional epidemiology and able to access MAT, ELISA, and PCR testing. During outbreaks, treatment is typically delivered through district hospitals coordinated with state public-health authorities.
hospitalCriteria: Access to PCR and IgM ELISA for leptospirosis
Capacity for intravenous antibiotics and acute supportive care
Renal replacement therapy on site or via rapid transfer
Critical-care capacity with mechanical ventilation for pulmonary hemorrhage
keyFacilities: ICMR-NIE Chennai and regional reference laboratories
Government tropical medicine and infectious disease departments
Tertiary critical care units capable of ECMO
Public-health emergency operations during monsoon season
preventionStrategies: Avoid walking, bathing, or swimming in floodwater, drains, paddy fields, or rivers in endemic regions during and after monsoon rains.
Wear waterproof boots, gloves, and protective clothing for unavoidable occupational water contact; cover cuts and abrasions before exposure.
Take doxycycline 200 mg orally once weekly during sustained high-risk exposure or as a single 200 mg dose after a defined accidental exposure (military, disaster response, athletes).
Reduce rodent populations through trash management, sealed grain storage, rat-proof housing, and integrated pest management.
Vaccinate cattle, pigs, and dogs against locally relevant Leptospira serovars; canine vaccines reduce zoonotic exposure in the household.
Drain stagnant water and chlorinate community water supplies after flooding.
Inform travellers planning freshwater adventures in the tropics about leptospirosis prevention and emergency contact for febrile illness.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full course of doxycycline or alternative antibiotic even after symptoms resolve.
Drink at least 2-3 L of fluids per day to support kidney recovery unless on dialysis with fluid restriction.
Avoid alcohol during and for 4 weeks after acute illness to support hepatic recovery.
Follow up at 4-6 weeks for liver, kidney, and ophthalmology assessment.
Report any eye redness, blurred vision, or photophobia weeks to months later — leptospiral uveitis can appear late.
Document exposure and case-confirmation details with public-health teams to support outbreak control.

recommended: Soft, easily digestible foods during acute febrile phase
Protein-rich foods (eggs, dairy, lentils) to support recovery
Iron- and folate-rich foods for patients with significant hematologic disturbance
Adequate hydration with oral rehydration solution or fruit juice unless on fluid restriction
avoid: Alcohol during acute illness and convalescence
Excess salt during fluid retention or active renal failure
Untreated freshwater and unfiltered ice from outdoor sources in endemic regions
Raw or undercooked animal products from unverified sources

exerciseGuidelines: Bed rest during acute febrile illness. Gradual return to activity over 2-4 weeks; severe disease with renal injury may require 8-12 weeks of restricted activity. Avoid contact sports until thrombocytopenia and coagulopathy resolve.
dailyManagement: Take antibiotics at exact prescribed times.
Monitor temperature, urine output, and weight daily during convalescence.
Avoid alcohol and hepatotoxic drugs during recovery.
Use mosquito and rodent control measures at home during ongoing outbreak.
Attend follow-up renal, hepatic, and ophthalmologic appointments.
Update tetanus immunization if exposure involved wounds in soil or floodwater.
prognosis: Mild anicteric leptospirosis has an excellent prognosis: most patients recover fully within 2-3 weeks with appropriate antibiotics, although fatigue can persist for several months. Weil's disease has a case-fatality rate of 5-40%, dominated by acute kidney injury, hemorrhage, and sepsis. Pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome carries the highest mortality — 50-75% — despite optimal critical care. Renal function recovers fully in 80-90% of survivors of acute kidney injury over weeks to months; chronic kidney disease is uncommon. Liver function normalizes within weeks. Leptospiral uveitis can be sight-threatening if untreated but responds to topical corticosteroids in most patients. Late complications include chronic fatigue, headache, mood changes, and isolated case reports of pancreatitis and myocarditis. Reinfection with a different serovar is possible because immunity is serovar-specific. Prevention through public-health measures, early diagnosis, and timely antibiotics has reduced outbreak mortality substantially when applied promptly.
recoveryTimeline: Acute febrile illness resolves within 5-10 days of antibiotic initiation in mild cases. Jaundice and renal injury (in severe disease) improve over 2-6 weeks. Full energy and stamina return within 2-3 months for most patients. Pulmonary hemorrhage survivors typically need 3-6 months for full lung-function recovery. Leptospiral uveitis can appear weeks to months after acute illness and may require months of topical treatment.
complications: Acute kidney injury requiring dialysis — present in 16-40% of severe cases.
Pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome with acute respiratory distress and high mortality.
Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia with cholestatic jaundice; usually reversible.
Thrombocytopenia with mucosal bleeding, gastrointestinal hemorrhage, or disseminated intravascular coagulation.
Aseptic meningitis or rarely meningoencephalitis with neurologic sequelae.
Leptospiral uveitis (anterior, intermediate, or pan-uveitis) weeks to months later.
Myocarditis with arrhythmias and conduction defects.
Rare reinfection by a different serovar.

supportResources.name: WHO Leptospirosis — Disease Outbreak Information
supportResources.description: WHO information on epidemiology, outbreaks, and control of leptospirosis.

supportResources.name: International Leptospirosis Society (ILS)
supportResources.description: Professional society publishing scientific resources, conference output, and guidelines on leptospirosis.

supportResources.name: CDC Leptospirosis
supportResources.description: Clinician-facing and traveler guidance from the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: Government of India — National Centre for Disease Control: Leptospirosis
supportResources.description: Indian national reporting, surveillance, and operational guidelines covering monsoon-season leptospirosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dengue Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both produce sudden fever, headache, and myalgia after exposure in tropical regions. Dengue causes retro-orbital pain, more prominent rash, thrombocytopenia with plasma leakage, and is diagnosed by NS1 antigen or PCR. Leptospirosis adds conjunctival suffusion, calf myalgia, jaundice, and renal injury and is treated with doxycycline.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause fever after tropical exposure. Malaria is diagnosed by blood film or rapid antigen test and treated with artemisinin. Leptospirosis lacks parasitemia and shows raised CK, conjunctival suffusion, and characteristic urinalysis or PCR results; co-infection is reported during monsoon outbreaks.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hepatitis B
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute hepatitis B causes jaundice but typically with transaminases 10-50× upper limit and milder bilirubin rise, with positive HBsAg and IgM anti-HBc. Leptospirosis (Weil's disease) shows transaminases under 5× upper limit, marked conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, renal failure, and characteristic exposure history.

confusedWithConditions.name: Typhoid fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Typhoid produces gradual rising fever with abdominal pain, hepatosplenomegaly, and rose spots over 1-3 weeks. Diagnosed by blood culture and treated with ceftriaxone or fluoroquinolone. Leptospirosis presents more abruptly with conjunctival suffusion, calf myalgia, and renal injury.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chikungunya
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chikungunya produces sudden fever with severe symmetrical joint pain (particularly small joints), rash, and IgM antibodies on serology. Joint pain is the dominant feature. Leptospirosis features more conjunctival suffusion, calf myalgia, and laboratory evidence of renal and hepatic involvement.

relatedConditions.name: Dengue Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Major monsoon-season differential and frequent coinfection in tropical Asia and Latin America.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-endemic febrile illness; both routinely tested in undifferentiated tropical fever.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis B
relatedConditions.relevance: Important differential for jaundice and acute liver involvement.

relatedConditions.name: Typhoid fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Common bacterial differential for monsoon-season fever and abdominal symptoms.

relatedConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another freshwater-exposure parasitic disease; relevant differential in returning travellers.

faqs.question: What is leptospirosis?
faqs.answer: Leptospirosis is a bacterial infection caused by Leptospira spirochetes transmitted to humans through contact with water, soil, or food contaminated by infected animal urine. Most cases are mild flu-like illnesses, but 5-10% develop severe Weil's disease with jaundice, renal failure, and bleeding, and a smaller fraction develop fatal pulmonary hemorrhage.

faqs.question: How is leptospirosis spread?
faqs.answer: Leptospirosis is spread by contact with water, soil, or animals contaminated with infected animal urine. Spirochetes enter through cuts, mucous membranes, or conjunctivae. Common exposures include flood water, paddy fields, rivers, sewers, and direct contact with rodents, cattle, pigs, or dogs. Human-to-human transmission is rare.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of leptospirosis?
faqs.answer: Symptoms begin 5-14 days after exposure and include sudden high fever, severe headache, intense calf and back pain, and bilateral red eyes (conjunctival suffusion) without discharge. Severe disease adds jaundice, reduced urine output, mucosal bleeding, and breathlessness from pulmonary involvement.

faqs.question: Is leptospirosis a serious disease?
faqs.answer: About 90% of cases are mild and self-limited. The remaining 5-10% develop severe disease with case-fatality rates of 5-40% in Weil's disease and 50-75% in pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome despite intensive care. Early antibiotic treatment dramatically reduces severity and mortality.

faqs.question: How is leptospirosis treated?
faqs.answer: Mild disease is treated with oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days, or amoxicillin or azithromycin in children and pregnancy. Severe disease is treated with intravenous penicillin G or ceftriaxone for 7 days plus aggressive supportive care, including dialysis for kidney injury and ventilation for pulmonary hemorrhage.

faqs.question: What is Weil's disease?
faqs.answer: Weil's disease is severe icteric leptospirosis: jaundice, acute kidney injury, and hemorrhage. It accounts for 5-10% of cases and carries 5-40% mortality. Hyperbilirubinemia is conjugated and renal failure is typically non-oliguric with hypokalemia. Early intravenous antibiotics and supportive care are essential.

faqs.question: How long does leptospirosis last?
faqs.answer: Mild leptospirosis resolves within 5-10 days of starting antibiotics, although fatigue may persist for weeks. Severe disease requires 1-3 weeks of hospitalization, with renal and hepatic recovery over weeks to months. Late complications such as uveitis can appear weeks to months after the acute illness.

faqs.question: Can leptospirosis be prevented?
faqs.answer: Yes. Avoid contact with floodwater and contaminated freshwater, wear waterproof boots and gloves for occupational water exposure, control rodents around homes, and vaccinate domestic animals. Doxycycline prophylaxis (200 mg weekly) protects high-risk responders, soldiers, and athletes during defined exposures.

faqs.question: Why is conjunctival suffusion important?
faqs.answer: Conjunctival suffusion — bilateral painless redness of the eyes without discharge — is one of the few highly characteristic clinical signs of leptospirosis, present in 30-90% of cases. It appears on day 2-4 of illness. Recognizing it in a febrile patient from an endemic region strongly raises suspicion and prompts empirical antibiotic therapy.

faqs.question: Is doxycycline effective for leptospirosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days is the standard first-line oral treatment for mild and moderate leptospirosis in non-pregnant adults and children over 8. It shortens illness duration by 2-3 days and reduces shedding. Treatment is most effective within 5 days of symptom onset.

faqs.question: Can leptospirosis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: True relapse is uncommon with appropriate antibiotic therapy. Reinfection with a different serovar is possible because immunity is serovar-specific. Late immune complications such as uveitis are not a relapse of bacterial infection but a delayed antibody-mediated reaction.

faqs.question: Is leptospirosis common in India?
faqs.answer: Yes. Leptospirosis is endemic across coastal and high-rainfall regions of India, with the heaviest documented burden in Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andaman & Nicobar Islands, Maharashtra, and Gujarat. Monsoon-season outbreaks recur each year, particularly during flooding.

faqs.question: How do you catch leptospirosis from rats?
faqs.answer: Rats and other rodents carry leptospires chronically in their kidneys and shed live organisms in urine for months or years. Humans become infected when broken skin or mucous membranes contact contaminated water, mud, or rarely the animals themselves. Direct rat-bite transmission is rare but possible.

faqs.question: What is the Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction?
faqs.answer: The Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction is a sudden worsening of symptoms — chills, fever, hypotension, worsening myalgia — within hours of the first dose of antibiotics in spirochetal infections. It occurs in 9-22% of leptospirosis cases and is managed with intravenous fluids, antipyretics, and short-course steroids in severe reactions.

faqs.question: Can swimming cause leptospirosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Swimming, wading, kayaking, or rafting in lakes, rivers, and floodwater in endemic regions can transmit leptospirosis, especially through abrasions or mucous membranes. The Borneo Eco-Challenge outbreak (2000) infected approximately 80 of 304 participants. Chlorinated pools and saltwater do not transmit infection.

faqs.question: What is leptospiral uveitis?
faqs.answer: Leptospiral uveitis is a late immune complication that appears weeks to months after acute infection. It causes eye redness, blurred vision, photophobia, and floaters. Most cases respond to topical corticosteroids and cycloplegics; rarely systemic immunosuppression is needed. Untreated severe uveitis can cause vision loss.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for leptospirosis?
faqs.answer: Human vaccines are available in a few countries (Cuba, France, China, Japan) but are not widely licensed; they protect against specific local serovars and require frequent boosters. Veterinary vaccines for cattle, pigs, and dogs are widely used and reduce zoonotic transmission.

faqs.question: Can leptospirosis be cured?
faqs.answer: Yes, when diagnosed and treated promptly. Most patients recover fully with antibiotics. Severe disease with renal failure or pulmonary hemorrhage requires intensive care but is also curable in survivors. Late complications such as uveitis are treatable but may require months of topical therapy.

faqs.question: What is the difference between leptospirosis and dengue?
faqs.answer: Both present with sudden fever, headache, and myalgia after monsoon-season exposure. Dengue causes retro-orbital pain, rash, plasma leakage, and is diagnosed by NS1 antigen or PCR. Leptospirosis adds conjunctival suffusion, calf myalgia, jaundice, and renal injury, and responds to doxycycline. Co-infection is well-documented during outbreaks.

faqs.question: Should I take antibiotics after a flood exposure?
faqs.answer: Single-dose doxycycline 200 mg taken within 24-72 hours of a defined high-risk exposure (such as wading through floodwater for hours) reduces clinical illness in randomized trials. Routine prophylaxis is not indicated for brief or low-risk exposures. Consult local public-health guidance during major flooding.

faqs.question: Why does leptospirosis cause muscle pain?
faqs.answer: Leptospires invade vascular endothelium and skeletal muscle, producing a vasculitis-like myositis that causes intense myalgia (often calf-predominant) and a marked rise in creatine kinase out of proportion to muscle injury. The pain is often the most prominent symptom and helps distinguish leptospirosis from other tropical fevers.

metaDescription: Leptospirosis and Weil's disease: spirochete infection from animal urine and floodwater. Symptoms, doxycycline/penicillin treatment and prevention reviewed.
